questionText: Disprove the statement: ``For all real numbers $a$ and $b$, if $a > b$ then $\dfrac{1}{a} < \dfrac{1}{b}$.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a conditional for all reals.
steps.explanation: The claim asserts the implication for *all* non-zero $a,b$; one pair with $a>b$ but $\tfrac1a\ge\tfrac1b$ disproves it.
We need a single pair satisfying the hypothesis but failing the conclusion.

steps.workingLatex: \text{reciprocal reverses order only within one sign}
steps.explanation: Taking reciprocals flips inequalities only when $a,b$ share a sign; straddling zero breaks the rule.
Here $a=1>b=-1$, so the hypothesis holds, but they lie on opposite sides of zero.
Indeed $1>-1$, so the hypothesis $a>b$ is genuinely satisfied by this pair.
Because $a$ is positive and $b$ negative, their reciprocals keep those signs, so $\tfrac1a>\tfrac1b$ — the opposite of the claim.
With $a=1>b=-1$ but $\tfrac1a = 1 > -1 = \tfrac1b$, the claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Take $a=1,\ b=-1$: then $a>b$, but $\tfrac1a = 1$ and $\tfrac1b = -1$, so $\tfrac1a < \tfrac1b$ fails. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
